I am writing to invite you.
Hello Doctor Lewis,
The meeting is on Friday at ten.

I am writing to invite you to our meeting.
The meeting is on Friday. I attach the agenda.
Graziele, could you write an email to Doctor Lewis, please?
Of course. I start with a greeting: Hello Doctor Lewis.
Good. Now the opening line. Why do you write?
I am writing to invite you to our training meeting.
Perfect. Add the day and the time in the body.
The meeting is on Friday at ten. I attach the agenda.
Now a small request. Ask her to confirm.
Could you confirm your presence, please?
Excellent. And how do you close the email?
Sincerely, Graziele. Thank you, Doctor Carter.

Hello Graziele. We have a training meeting on Friday at ten. Could you write a formal email to Doctor Lewis, please? Invite her to the meeting. Please attach the agenda, and ask her to confirm. Start with a greeting and finish with Sincerely and your name. Thank you so much.

Hi Graziele, this is Ana, a quick tip about formal emails. A good email has four parts. First, a greeting, like Hello Doctor Lewis. Then, an opening line, like I am writing to invite you. Next, a short body with the day and time. Last, a closing, like Sincerely, and your name. Short, clear, and polite. You can do it!

Graziele writes a formal email to Doctor Lewis. First, she writes the greeting: Hello Doctor Lewis. Then, the opening line: I am writing to invite you. Next, the body with the day and time. After that, the request: Could you confirm? Finally, the closing: Sincerely, Graziele.
